ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for bibimbap bowl with cheese and beef specifically; mapped to broad 'beef dish' class as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for bibimbap-style beef bowl with cheese; A0FJP (beef-based composite dish) used as closest defensible parent code. No exact or analogous code available for this fusion composite.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g (one bowl), as typical for a Japanese beef rice bowl served at Sukiya restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Melted Cheese Beef Bibimbap Bowl — Sukiya Singapore nutritional data via HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific Sukiya Singapore menu item (cr_id G00010190). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI ~64 used as dominant carbohydrate component anchor
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. The dominant carbohydrate source is Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~64, University of Sydney GI Database). The dish contains 8.85 g carbohydrate per 100 g; serving size 400 g yields ~35.4 g carbohydrate. High fat (23 g/100g) and protein (16 g/100g) content will attenuate postprandial glycemic response, so GI 64 is a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (64 × 35.4) / 100 = 22.7, rounded to 22.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; a single 400 g bowl delivers approximately 5116 mg sodium, exceeding the entire daily recommended limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish or consume only a very small portion (≤50 g) under medical supervision, as the extreme sodium load from soy-braised beef, kimchi, gochujang, and melted cheese poses a serious risk of acute blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 8.85 g/100 g and sugar at 6.24 g/100 g; however, the high fat content (23 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity, and gochujang contributes added sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate and sugar intake per serving, request reduced gochujang to lower added sugar, and be aware that the high fat content may blunt but also prolong postprandial glycemic response; the very high sodium level is an additional comorbidity risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (78 mg/100 g), and extreme sodium (1279 mg/100 g) collectively represent a significant cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium, all of which contribute to atherogenesis, endothelial dysfunction, and elevated blood pressure; if consumed, portion size must be strictly limited and frequency minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (arachidonic acid source), estimated saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, sodium 1279 mg/100 g, and nightshade-derived gochujang are all pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as the combination of red meat, trans fat, high sodium, and nightshade-derived chili in gochujang drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways and worsens joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Melted Cheese Beef Bibimbap Bowl is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served at Sukiya, a Japanese gyudon chain operating in Singapore. The dish features Sukiya's signature simmered beef (gyudon-style, slow-cooked in a sweet-savoury soy-mirin broth) layered over 100% Japan-harvested short-grain white rice, topped with shredded and melted cheese, seasoned bean sprouts, seasoned spinach (namul), kimchi, gochujang (a Korean sweet-spicy fermented red pepper paste), and a half-boiled egg. The flavour profile is rich, umami-forward, and moderately spicy, with the melted cheese adding creaminess and the kimchi providing fermented tang. Texturally, the dish combines soft rice, tender braised beef, silky egg yolk, and slightly crunchy bean sprouts. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (16.1 g/100 g), very high in fat (23.1 g/100 g) including significant saturated fat, and extremely high in sodium (1279 mg/100 g), making it a high-calorie, high-sodium option. Calcium is notably high (1375 mg/100 g) due to the melted cheese component. Carbohydrate is relatively low at 8.85 g/100 g per the HPB database entry. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients due to extreme sodium and saturated fat loads, and requires careful portion control for Type 2 Diabetes patients due to added sugars in gochujang and the overall energy density.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Sukiya cheese beef bibimbap?
Is melted cheese beef bibimbap high in calories?
What are the ingredients in Sukiya bibimbap?
Is Sukiya bibimbap suitable for hypertension?
Cheese beef bibimbap nutrition facts Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this exact Sukiya Singapore menu item (cr_id G00010190) with HPB-assigned confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from beef and cheese composition using USDA FoodData Central analogous data as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this item.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source and is plausible given the low vegetable-to-rice ratio in the as-served portion analysed. 2. saturated_fat_g (~8.5 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (~0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for simmered beef and processed cheese components, as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this item; these are approximate values. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this dish and not available from a single approved-source equivalent for this exact composite; USDA FoodData Central consulted but no exact composite match found. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported fields not available in the HPB Foodo database for this specific item; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this composite dish. 5. GI is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 64, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate source; actual GI of the composite dish has not been directly measured. 6. Calcium value of 1375 mg/100 g is unusually high and reflects the HPB-reported figure; this is plausible if the cheese component is very high-calcium (e.g., parmesan-style) or if the HPB figure includes a measurement artefact; flagged for downstream review. 7. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine (Sukiya is a Japanese chain) with Korean-influenced ingredients (bibimbap, kimchi, gochujang); Japanese naming rule applied. 8. ontology.foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as 'analogous' is not a permitted value; A0FJP is the closest parent code available in FoodEx2 for this composite beef dish.
